content.text: The service catalog is only as useful as the metadata it contains. A generic Backstage catalog entry with a name and owner tells a platform team nothing about the AI risks, data handling requirements, or operational dependencies of a GenAI service. A domain-specific taxonomy — a controlled vocabulary of component types and a standard annotation schema — makes the catalog queryable, drives automated scorecard checks, and enables cross-team discovery of reusable capabilities.
Every entry in the catalog is defined by a
file at the root of the service repository. Backstage's entity model has five standard kinds; GenAI services are always
entities, but they belong to a
What it represents in a GenAI context
A business line or functional area that owns GenAI capabilities
A coherent product or feature set composed of multiple components
A single deployable service, model, or pipeline
A typed interface a component exposes
entity — not an individual. If the owning team dissolves, the component is reassigned to the platform team until a new owner is designated. Unowned components are blocked from production deployment.
field uses free-form strings, which quickly becomes noisy. The GenAI catalog policy mandates that the
annotation carries one of the controlled values below. Backstage's catalog API is then queryable by this annotation, enabling platform-level dashboards and policy rules.
A service that retrieves context from a vector store and augments LLM prompts with it
A service running a multi-step reasoning loop that invokes tools
A reverse proxy that routes, rate-limits, and observes all LLM API calls
A service that converts text to dense vectors for downstream search or clustering
A custom-trained or LoRA-adapted model hosted internally
A scheduled pipeline that evaluates model or RAG quality against a golden dataset
A versioned repository of reusable prompt templates consumed by other services
The following example shows a complete
for a RAG application. Fields marked with a comment are required by the GenAI catalog policy; the rest are optional but encouraged.

apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: product-search-rag
  title: Product Search RAG API
  description: |
    Retrieval-augmented search over the product catalogue.
    Returns ranked results with cited source documents.
  annotations:
    # ── GenAI required ────────────────────────────────────
    genai.io/type:           rag-application          # required
    genai.io/primary-model:  gpt-4o-mini              # required
    genai.io/model-tier:     standard                 # required
    genai.io/data-class:     internal                 # required
    genai.io/pii-in-prompts: "false"                  # required
    genai.io/golden-path:    rag                      # required
    genai.io/eval-suite-url: https://grafana.internal/d/rag-eval/product-search
    genai.io/cost-center:    CC-4821                  # required
    # ── GenAI optional (rag-application) ──────────────────
    genai.io/vector-store:   pgvector
    genai.io/chunk-size:     "512"
    genai.io/retrieval-k:    "20"
    genai.io/cache-strategy: semantic
    # ── Standard Backstage ────────────────────────────────
    github.com/project-slug: acme/product-search-rag
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.
  tags:
    - genai
    - rag
    - product
    - enterprise-delivery
  links:
    - url:   https://grafana.internal/d/genai/product-search-rag
      title: Service Dashboard
      icon:  dashboard
    - url:   https://runbooks.internal/product-search-rag
      title: Runbook
      icon:  article
spec:
  type:      service
  lifecycle:  production
  owner:     group:search-team
  system:    product-discovery
  dependsOn:
    - resource:default/pgvector-cluster
    - component:default/embedding-service
  providesApis:
    - product-search-openapi-v2
The platform team ships a catalog-info linter as a GitHub Actions step. It runs on every PR that touches
and fails if any required annotation is missing or set to an invalid value. This prevents incomplete catalog entries from reaching production.

# .github/workflows/catalog-lint.yml
name: Catalog Lint
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "catalog-info.yaml"

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install pyyaml
      - run: python scripts/lint-catalog-info.py catalog-info.yaml
Data Classification and Its Catalog Impact

annotation drives a set of downstream platform controls. Setting the wrong value is a policy violation; the scorecard verifies consistency between the annotation and the actual data sources consumed by the service.
Service only handles publicly available data; no PII
Standard audit logging; no additional DLP scan
Service handles internal business data not available publicly
Structured log retention capped at 30 days; DLP scan on completions
Service handles trade secrets, financial data, or pre-release information
No prompt text logged; completions reviewed by output filter; SIEM routing mandatory
Service handles data subject to regulatory controls (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI)
Full PII scrubbing enforced; data residency policy applied; DPO review required before first production deployment
, the scorecard will flag an inconsistency — public data should not contain PII. The CI linter catches the most common mismatches; the scorecard catches runtime drift.
Once metadata is standardised, the catalog becomes a discovery tool. Teams can query the Backstage API to find reusable components before building new ones.

# Find all production RAG applications in the product-discovery system
curl -s "https://backstage.internal/api/catalog/entities?kind=Component&filter=metadata.annotations.genai.io/type=rag-application,spec.lifecycle=production" \
  | jq '.[] | {name: .metadata.name, owner: .spec.owner, model: .metadata.annotations["genai.io/primary-model"]}'

# Output:
# {
#   "name": "product-search-rag",
#   "owner": "group:search-team",
#   "model": "gpt-4o-mini"
# }
# {
#   "name": "faq-rag-api",
#   "owner": "group:support-ai",
#   "model": "gpt-4o-mini"
# }
Platform-level aggregations built on the same API power the GenAI cost dashboard (summing
across components), the model inventory report (aggregating
for contract negotiations), and the data-class risk register (listing all
Systems and Domains group related components. They are also defined in
repository maintained by the platform team. GenAI domains carry an annotation indicating the primary data governance policy.

# Domain entry — one per business line
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Domain
metadata:
  name: customer-intelligence
  annotations:
    genai.io/data-governance-policy: gdpr-eu        # policy applied to all child systems
    genai.io/ai-risk-classification: high            # drives review cadence
spec:
  owner: group:platform-ai

---
# System entry — one per product or feature set
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: System
metadata:
  name: contact-centre-ai
  description: AI capabilities powering the contact centre
  annotations:
    genai.io/monthly-token-budget-usd: "5000"       # cost cap across the system
spec:
  owner:  group:cx-ai-team
  domain: customer-intelligence
Infrastructure that GenAI components depend on — vector stores, GPU node pools, object storage for training data — should be registered as
entities. Typed API entities enable automated API documentation and contract testing.

# Resource entry — pgvector cluster
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Resource
metadata:
  name: pgvector-cluster
  annotations:
    genai.io/resource-type:  vector-store
    genai.io/engine:         pgvector
    genai.io/dimension:      "1536"            # text-embedding-3-small output size
    genai.io/index-type:     hnsw
    genai.io/data-class:     internal
spec:
  type:  database
  owner: group:platform-data
  system: product-discovery

---
# API entry — OpenAPI spec for the RAG search endpoint
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: API
metadata:
  name: product-search-openapi-v2
  annotations:
    genai.io/api-auth-scheme: bearer-oidc
    genai.io/rate-limit-rpm:  "120"
spec:
  type:       openapi
  lifecycle:   production
  owner:      group:search-team
  system:     product-discovery
  definition:
    $text: ./openapi.yaml        # resolved by TechDocs at build time
Dependency Relations and the Catalog Graph
fields build an explicit dependency graph that Backstage renders as a visual map. For GenAI services this graph is critical for incident response: knowing that
tells the on-call engineer exactly what to check when the RAG API degrades.
Hard runtime dependency — component cannot function without the listed entity
Component calls the listed API at runtime
Component belongs to the named System
The dependency graph enables the platform's cascade alert rule: when a
entity (e.g. the pgvector cluster) triggers a high-severity alert, PagerDuty automatically notifies the owners of all
. Teams do not need to manually subscribe to infrastructure alerts.

The platform team runs a nightly report against the Backstage catalog API that measures metadata completeness across all registered GenAI components. The report feeds the Backstage scorecard and a Slack digest sent to

import httpx, json

BACKSTAGE = "https://backstage.internal/api/catalog"
REQUIRED_ANNOTATIONS = [
    "genai.io/type", "genai.io/primary-model", "genai.io/model-tier",
    "genai.io/data-class", "genai.io/pii-in-prompts",
    "genai.io/golden-path", "genai.io/cost-center",
]

def fetch_genai_components() -> list[dict]:
    resp = httpx.get(
        f"{BACKSTAGE}/entities",
        params={"kind": "Component", "filter": "metadata.tags=genai"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {BACKSTAGE_TOKEN}"},
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

def completeness_report() -> dict:
    components = fetch_genai_components()
    results = []
    for c in components:
        annots = c.get("metadata", {}).get("annotations", {})
        missing = [k for k in REQUIRED_ANNOTATIONS if k not in annots]
        results.append({
            "name":    c["metadata"]["name"],
            "owner":   c["spec"].get("owner"),
            "missing": missing,
            "score":   round((len(REQUIRED_ANNOTATIONS) - len(missing))
                             / len(REQUIRED_ANNOTATIONS) * 100),
        })
    results.sort(key=lambda r: r["score"])
    return {
        "total":    len(results),
        "complete": sum(1 for r in results if r["score"] == 100),
        "worst":    results[:5],   # bottom 5 for Slack digest
    }
A GenAI service catalog is only as powerful as its taxonomy and metadata standards. Defining a controlled vocabulary of component types through

, requiring a standard annotation set on every component, and enforcing those requirements with a CI linter transforms the catalog from a static directory into a queryable, policy-enforcing, cross-team discovery layer. System and Domain entries with governance annotations group components into manageable units with shared cost budgets and risk classifications. Resource and API entities make the runtime dependency graph explicit, enabling cascade alerting and impact analysis during incidents. The nightly completeness report closes the loop by surfacing gaps to component owners before they become scorecard violations.
